file_name: Miller et al. - 2023 - Implementation of artificial intelligence for the detection of cutaneous melanoma within a primary c.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a commercially available AI-powered software (FotoFinder Vexia) embedded in a digital dermatoscope to assess the predictive risk of malignant melanoma (MM) in outdoor enthusiasts. The AI utilized a deep-learning algorithm and complex machine learning to generate predictive scores for queried pigmented lesions, which were then evaluated against histopathology ground truth to determine sensitivity (53.33%), specificity (54.44%), and accuracy (54.17%).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes a cross-sectional study involving 423 participants, including surveys, whole-body skin cancer screening, and the evaluation of a commercially available AI-powered software's performance (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) against histopathology ground truth. It reports original experimental results and prevalence data rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Implementation of artificial intelligence for the detection of cutaneous melanoma within a primary care setting: prevalence and types of skin cancer in outdoor enthusiasts
brief_summary: This cross-sectional study evaluated the prevalence of skin cancer and the performance of a commercially available AI-powered dermatoscope (FotoFinder Vexia) in 423 outdoor enthusiasts in Australia. The AI system, utilizing deep learning convolutional neural networks, assessed the predictive risk of malignant melanoma in queried pigmented lesions against histopathology ground truth. The study found notably higher rates of keratinocyte carcinomas and malignant melanoma in surfers, swimmers, and walkers/runners compared to the general population. However, the AI software demonstrated lower-than-expected diagnostic performance, with a sensitivity of 53.33%, specificity of 54.44%, and accuracy of 54.17%. The authors concluded that while AI shows promise, caution is warranted when using this specific version for patients with potentially sun-damaged skin, as its performance may be limited by training data biases.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target of AI detection and prevalence study. Histopathology confirmed 15 cases among queried lesions. Subtypes included superficial spreading (n=1), lentigo maligna (n=5), lentiginous (n=3), and not otherwise specified (n=6). Invasiveness classified as in-situ (n=4), invasive (n=1), or both (n=10).
Histopathology confirmed 33 benign lesions among queried lesions. Used as negative controls for AI sensitivity/specificity calculation.
25 out of 33 benign lesions were confirmed as naevus derivatives.
3 benign lesions were classified as pigmented seborrheic keratosis.
2 benign lesions were classified as dermatofibroma.

Mentioned as a prevalent skin cancer type (BCC and SCC). Study concluded rates were notably higher in outdoor enthusiasts compared to the general population, but specific counts for KC were not detailed in the queried lesion subset.

all_task_types.target: Malignant melanoma (MM) vs. benign pigmented lesions
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task where the AI system (FotoFinder Vexia/Moleanalyzer-Pro) predicts the risk of malignant melanoma in queried pigmented lesions. The model outputs a continuous predictive score (0.00–1.00) which is discretized into categories: 'unsuspicious' (0.00–0.20), 'requiring clarification' (0.21–0.49), and 'suspicious' (0.50–1.00). Performance was evaluated using sensitivity (53.33%), specificity (54.44%), and accuracy (54.17%) against histopathology ground truth.

datasets_used.name: Primary Care Clinical Cohort (Outdoor Enthusiasts)
datasets_used.description: A cross-sectional clinical cohort of 423 participants (surfers, swimmers, and walkers/runners) recruited in Australia for a skin cancer screening study. The dataset consists of clinical examinations and dermoscopic images of queried pigmented lesions captured using a digital dermatoscope (Medicam) embedded with AI software (FotoFinder Vexia/Moleanalyzer-Pro). Histopathology from a commercial laboratory served as the ground truth for the queried lesions.

use.testing: The 48 queried lesions (15 MM, 33 benign) were used to evaluate the diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC) of the commercially available AI software against histopathology ground truth.

datasets_used.name: FotoFinder Vexia/Moleanalyzer-Pro Internal Reference Database
datasets_used.description: The proprietary database of reference images used to train the deep-learning convolutional neural network (CNN) embedded in the FotoFinder Vexia software. The paper notes this database was developed in Germany and likely trained on non-sun-damaged skin, which may have contributed to the lower-than-expected performance in this study's population.

use.training: Used to train the AI algorithm (CNN) prior to the study.
use.other: The study authors speculate this dataset's composition (non-sun-damaged skin) limited the AI's generalizability to the sun-damaged skin of the study participants.

hypothesis_testing.test: Independent sample t-tests, Chi-square tests, ANOVA (with Bonferroni post-hoc test), and bivariate (two-tailed) Pearson correlation coefficient.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the number of skin cancers or demographic/behavioral variables between the groups (surfers, swimmers, walkers/runners) or genders, and no association between the frequency of skin malignancies, age, and body mass index.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the number of skin cancers or demographic/behavioral variables between the groups (surfers, swimmers, walkers/runners) or genders, and there is an association between the frequency of skin malignancies, age, and body mass index.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined by p-values less than 0.05. Specific significant findings included differences in age, mass, body surface area, experience, hours/week, weeks/year, total hours/year, lifetime hours, and activity during peak UV between groups, as well as differences in prevention strategies (clothing, zinc use, sunscreen reapplication) and AK prevalence between genders.

metric: Confusion Matrix Counts (TP, TN, FP, FN)
Primary Care Clinical Cohort (Outdoor En.notes: The study evaluated 48 queried pigmented lesions (15 histopathology-confirmed malignant melanomas and 33 benign lesions) from 423 participants. The AI system used a threshold of >0.50 for positive prediction. The authors noted lower-than-expected performance compared to previous studies (e.g., MacLellan et al., 2021 reported sensitivity 88.1% and specificity 78.8% for the same device in a non-clinical setting), potentially due to the algorithm being trained on non-sun-damaged skin while this cohort had sun-damaged skin.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on a large database of reference images developed in Germany; likely trained on non-sun-damaged skin.

architecture_details.description: Commercially available AI-powered software embedded in a digital dermatoscope (Medicam). Utilizes a conventional neural network for skin cancer examinations. The algorithm relies on a large database of reference images and uses complex machine learning to continually update and increase sensitivity and specificity.

training_details.training_data_source: Proprietary internal reference database of reference images (developed in Germany).
training_details.training_data_characteristics: Likely composed of non-sun-damaged skin, which may have limited generalizability to sun-damaged skin populations.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Continuous predictive score ranging from 0.00 to 1.00

conclusions: Rates of both keratinocyte carcinomas and malignant melanoma were notably higher in aquatic and land-based outdoor enthusiasts compared to the general Australian population.
These findings highlight the clinical importance of sun-safe protection measures and regular skin screening in individuals who spend significant time outdoors.
The use of AI in the early identification of malignant melanoma is promising.
The AI software used in this study demonstrated lower-than-expected performance metrics (sensitivity 53.33%, specificity 54.44%, accuracy 54.17%).

Reservations should be held before recommending this particular version of this AI software as a reliable adjunct for clinicians in skin imaging diagnostics in patients with potentially sun-damaged skin.
important_remarks: The AI software (FotoFinder Vexia) demonstrated significantly lower-than-expected diagnostic performance in this clinical setting, with a sensitivity of 53.33%, specificity of 54.44%, and accuracy of 54.17%, which is well below the mean sensitivity (84.2%) and specificity (89.1%) reported in a systematic review of 197 studies.

The study identifies a potential population bias in the AI's training data; experts speculate the algorithm was trained on non-sun-damaged skin (developed in Germany), leading to poor performance on the sun-damaged skin prevalent in the Australian outdoor enthusiast cohort.

The study has a small sample size for the AI evaluation, consisting of only 48 queried lesions (15 malignant melanomas and 33 benign lesions), which limits the statistical power of the performance metrics.
There is a risk of selection bias because only one specialist identified the lesions of interest for AI querying, rather than a multi-reader consensus or independent validation.

The AI system had physical capture limitations, failing to image lesions on hard-to-reach areas (e.g., helix/antihelix of the ear) or those obscured by tattoo pigmentations, introducing potential exclusion bias.
The study highlights a gap in real-world clinical validation, noting that most prior high-performing AI studies utilized curated image datasets rather than real-world primary care data.

The authors conclude that reservations should be held regarding the use of this specific version of the AI software as a reliable adjunct for clinicians diagnosing patients with potentially sun-damaged skin.
